nativeRange.habitat: Found on reef slopes and walls around the Ryukyu Islands and Bonin Islands in subtropical marine environments.

diet.notes: Primarily planktivorous in the wild; generally accepts frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and other meaty zooplankton preparations in captivity. Has been documented also consuming small amounts of filamentous algae. Tends to do best with frequent small feedings (2–3 times daily) rather than single large meals.

summary: The Pink Anthias is a smaller reef fish from subtropical Japanese waters, best kept in groups of one male and several females. It is a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning females can change sex to male — and requires frequent small feedings to stay in good condition.
careNotes:
The Pink Anthias (*Pseudanthias taira*) is a reef-associated species endemic to the subtropical Northwest Pacific, found around the Ryukyu Islands and Bonin Islands of Japan. It is a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning females can transition to male if the dominant male is lost — and groups are best kept as one male with several females. Keeping multiple males together tends to result in significant aggression between them.

Like most anthias, the Pink Anthias benefits from frequent small feedings — two to three times daily — rather than a single large meal. In the wild the species feeds on zooplankton (tiny drifting animals), and captive specimens generally accept frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and similar meaty preparations. Newly acquired fish can sometimes take several days to begin accepting frozen foods, so offering live or freshly thawed foods during the acclimation period is often advisable.

The species is generally considered reef-safe and tends to coexist with corals and most peaceful invertebrates without issue. It is not commonly encountered in the aquarium trade and may require patience to source from a specialty retailer. The Pink Anthias belongs to the family Anthiadidae, a grouping that was formerly folded into the broader sea bass family Serranidae but is now recognised as a distinct family in current classifications.
breedingNotes: The Pink Anthias is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning individuals begin life as females and the most dominant fish in a group will eventually transition to male. If the dominant male is removed or dies, the largest remaining female will typically begin transitioning to male over a period of days to weeks. Successful captive breeding has not been well-documented for this species, and aquarium spawning of anthias in general is considered an advanced undertaking.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, taxonomy (family Anthiadidae), adult size (max 10 cm TL), and native range. FishBase restricts the range to Northwest Pacific: Japan (Ryukyu Islands, Bonin Islands), contradicting LiveAquaria's broader Indo-Pacific/Red Sea description. Adult size averaged: LA ~12.7 cm vs FishBase 10 cm TL → used 6–11 cm range. Family updated to Anthiadidae per current classification.

additional.name: FishBase Country List
additional.notes: Confirmed native range: Japan, Bonin Islands, Ryukyu Islands only (3 territories, all Northwest Pacific).

fish.breedingNotes: The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite, with sex change from female to male occurring when the dominant male is lost. Captive breeding has rarely been documented.
